Instead of a script that manually calls each stage in sequence, you define what a training run consists of — data source, architecture config, optimizer parameters, evaluation criteria — and a runner resolves and executes the dependency graph. Tools in the MLOps ecosystem already support this pattern. The practical benefit is that you can diff two experiment definitions the same way you diff two Terraform plans: before you run anything, you can see exactly what changed and what did not.

If someone on your team left tomorrow, could any other engineer re-run last month's training job and get the same model weights? If the honest answer is no, that is the gap Model Training as Code is designed to close. Start by co-versioning your configs with your model code in Git. Everything else builds from there.
content.label: Engineering Takeaway

content.text: Why This Takes Time to Become Standard

Infrastructure-as-code did not become the default in a year. It took a decade of production incidents, team turnover, and accumulated pain before Terraform became table stakes for any serious engineering org. The same trajectory is ahead for training pipelines, but ML teams are arguably on an accelerated timeline because the pressure from production deployments is arriving faster than it did for general infrastructure. Teams shipping models at scale — where a regression in a production model has real downstream consequences — are already living these practices. The teams still running training like it is 2018 will get there through necessity, not enthusiasm. The argument for moving sooner is simply that the debugging, onboarding, and auditing costs compound quietly for every month the structural debt is left in place.

title: Give Your Coding Agent the Decisions Your Team Made
dek: Lore surfaces a real production gap: coding agents are stateless by default, and that statelessness will confidently undo your best architectural choices. Here's the engineering pattern that fixes it.
excerpt: Coding agents see your files. They don't see your reasoning. That gap is why an agent will swap your event-driven design for polling, re-introduce a dependency you explicitly ripped out, or contradict a security call made after a real incident. None of it is the model's fault — it has no signal that a decision was deliberate.

content.alt: Give Your Coding Agent the Decisions Your Team Made

content.text: A project called Lore appeared on Hacker News with a pitch that is almost uncomfortably simple: give your coding agent the decisions your team already made. The repo is github.com/itsthelore/rac-core, and the moment I read the description I felt that particular kind of recognition you get when someone names a problem you have been quietly losing time to. Coding agents are stateless by default. That is not a bug in any individual tool — it is an architectural reality of how every session is constructed. And it is, right now, one of the sharpest edges in production agentic development.
The Statelessness Problem Is Not About Hallucination

When people say their coding agent made a weird or wrong change, the instinct is to blame the model. But most of the time the model is doing exactly what a competent engineer would do if you handed them the codebase with zero context about its history. It sees polling and event-driven logic coexisting in adjacent files and picks the simpler-looking one. It spots a library in an old branch and helpfully reintroduces it, unaware you spent two weeks ripping it out because of a licensing conflict. It restructures an auth flow without knowing that the current shape was the result of a post-incident review.

These are not hallucinations. Hallucination is the model fabricating facts that do not exist. This is something structurally different: the model reasoning competently over incomplete context and arriving at a locally coherent but globally wrong answer. The distinction matters because the fix is also different. You do not need a better model. You need better context.
The Architecture: RAG for Institutional Memory

The pattern Lore is pushing is retrieval-augmented generation applied not to documentation or codebases but to the decision log of a team. If you have built RAG for a knowledge base, the mechanics are familiar. The novel part is the source material and the discipline required to produce it. Let me walk through the four layers as I would think about them in a real system.

The capture layer is where most teams will fail first. You need decisions in a structured, retrievable form. That means Architecture Decision Records written as markdown files versioned in the repo, PR descriptions that actually explain the why rather than the what, and any significant calls recorded in Notion or Confluence exported and normalized. The format matters less than the habit. A flat JSON object with fields for context, decision, consequences, and domain tags is enough to get started.

content.code:
{
  "id": "adr-0042",
  "title": "Use event-driven messaging over polling for order state changes",
  "status": "accepted",
  "domain": ["orders", "infra"],
  "context": "Polling the orders table every 5s was causing lock contention at peak load.",
  "decision": "Publish state change events to an internal message bus. Consumers subscribe per domain.",
  "consequences": "Eventual consistency in downstream reads. Polling-based consumers must be migrated.",
  "do_not_revert_because": "Lock contention issue is still present. Polling will not scale past current traffic."
}

content.text: The retrieval layer embeds those decision records and performs semantic search at the start of each agent session or tool call. When the agent is about to touch the orders module, you retrieve the top-k decisions tagged to that domain or semantically similar to the task description, then inject them into context before the agent generates any code. The embedding and retrieval can be done with any standard vector store. The query is usually a combination of the file paths in scope and a short natural language description of the task.

The injection layer prepends the retrieved decisions to the system prompt or, if the agent supports it, passes them as a structured tool response before code generation begins. This is where the framing matters. You are not dumping raw ADRs at the model. You are presenting them as authoritative constraints: these are decisions this team has already made, do not reverse them without explicit instruction. That framing changes how the model weighs the retrieved context against its priors.

The scope control layer keeps retrieval precise. Tagging decisions by domain — auth, infra, API contracts, data models — means a task touching the payments module does not drown in decisions about the notification service. Noisy retrieval is its own failure mode. If every session dumps thirty irrelevant ADRs into context, engineers will stop writing decisions down because they will see no effect on agent behavior.
What Makes This Hard in Practice

The hard part of this system is not the retrieval pipeline. Embedding documents and running semantic search is a solved problem with well-understood tooling. The hard part is the discipline to write decisions down at the moment they are made, with enough context that a retrieval system can surface them usefully six months later.
content.label: The Real Constraint

content.text: Most teams have an implicit decision log that lives in the heads of two or three senior engineers. It works until one of them leaves, or until an agent joins the codebase. Agents cannot ask clarifying questions the way a new hire would. They will not flag uncertainty about whether a pattern is intentional. They will just act. The cost of undocumented decisions goes up significantly when agents are in the loop.

There is also a retrieval quality problem worth taking seriously. Semantic search over decision logs is imprecise enough that you will sometimes surface irrelevant decisions and miss critical ones. Hybrid retrieval — combining embedding similarity with keyword matching on domain tags and affected file paths — tends to perform better than pure semantic search for this use case. It is worth investing in the tagging schema early, because retrofitting structure onto a large decision log is painful.
The Engineering Takeaway

If you are building with coding agents today — Claude Code, Cursor, Copilot, or anything downstream of a code-generation model — the question to ask is not whether the model is good enough. It probably is. The question is whether you have given it the context it needs to make decisions that align with your system's history. Statelessness is the default. Persistent institutional context is something you have to engineer.

Lore is an early implementation of a pattern that I expect to become standard in any serious agentic development workflow. The capture and retrieval mechanics are not complex. The cultural shift — treating architectural decisions as first-class engineering artifacts that belong in the repo alongside the code they govern — is the actual work. Start there. The retrieval pipeline will be the easy part.

title: Claude Code MRI Second Opinion — Agentic Boundaries
dek: When a coding agent walks someone through their MRI findings and gets 535 Hacker News comments, that is not a curiosity. That is a forcing function for every builder working on agentic systems.
excerpt: Someone fed their MRI to Claude Code — a coding agent — and came away feeling like they had a genuine second opinion. The HN thread blew up for good reason. This moment exposes something most teams building agentic systems have not yet reckoned with: the label on the box is not a capability ceiling, and without deliberate architectural guardrails, your agent will wander into domains you never tested, with stakes you never modeled.

content.alt: Claude Code MRI Second Opinion — Agentic Boundaries

content.text: Someone fed their MRI to Claude Code — a tool built to read codebases, write diffs, and run shell commands — walked through the findings in a long iterative session, and came away feeling like they had received a genuine second opinion. The post landed 411 points and 535 comments on Hacker News. That volume is not noise. It is a signal about where agentic AI is right now, and more importantly, where most teams building on top of it are not.
The Label on the Box Is a UX Layer, Not a Capability Ceiling

Claude Code is marketed and scaffolded as a coding agent. Its defaults lean toward repository traversal, shell execution, and diff generation. But when you hand any capable LLM a long context window, a tool-use scaffold, and the ability to reason step-by-step across modalities, you have not built a coding tool. You have built a general reasoning engine wearing a coding tool's jacket.

The MRI session works architecturally for the same reason a RAG pipeline over legal documents works: image bytes get tokenized or described, text context accumulates across turns, the model issues clarifying questions, and the loop tightens iteratively toward an answer. That pattern is domain-agnostic. The primitives — multimodal input, extended context, chain-of-thought, tool invocation — do not care whether the artifact under analysis is a TypeScript monorepo or a T2-weighted brain scan. The scaffold is the same. The stakes are not.
Where the Agentic Loop Actually Lives

content.alt: Where the Agentic Loop Actually Lives

content.text: It helps to be precise about what an agentic loop is at the mechanical level. At its core it is a while loop: the model observes state, picks an action, executes it via a tool or produces text, updates context, and repeats until a stopping condition is met. In a coding agent the actions are things like reading a file, running a test suite, or writing a patch. But nothing in that loop specification constrains the domain. The constraint is only what tools you expose and what system prompt you set.

content.code:
while not done:
    observation = get_current_context()   # file tree, scan image, legal doc — same call
    action = model.decide(observation)    # model does not know your intended domain
    result = tool_executor.run(action)    # only constrained by tools you registered
    context.append(result)

content.text: This is not a flaw. Generality is the product. But it means the domain wall you think you have drawn in your product spec does not exist at the loop level. Users will find the edge, and they will push through it, because the model will respond helpfully rather than refuse. That is the behavior you trained for.
Hallucination Risk Changes Category at Domain Boundaries

content.alt: Hallucination Risk Changes Category at Domain Boundaries

content.text: In a software context, a hallucinated function signature or a fabricated API method is annoying and correctable. You run the tests. The stack trace tells you what is wrong. The feedback loop is tight and the cost of failure is low. In a medical imaging context, a confident-sounding miss — a finding described with appropriate hedging language that nonetheless points the reader in the wrong direction — belongs to a different failure category entirely. The feedback loop is slow, the reader may lack the expertise to calibrate the model's confidence, and the downstream action could be a real clinical decision.

The problem is not that the model is uniquely bad at medical reasoning. The problem is that our standard eval pipelines were never designed to catch this boundary case. We measure pass-at-k on coding benchmarks. We measure retrieval precision on document QA. We do not have robust runtime evals that fire when an agent session drifts into an unintended domain mid-conversation. That gap is now a product risk, not just a research problem.

Scope creep at runtime is not a user education problem. Users will always push agents into adjacent domains. If your system has no mechanism to detect that drift and adjust behavior accordingly, your safety assumptions are built on a prompt you wrote once and never tested at the boundary.
content.label: Engineering Reality

content.text: Engineering the Domain Wall You Do Not Get for Free

The architectural implication is straightforward to state and genuinely hard to implement well. You have to engineer the boundary deliberately because nothing in the underlying scaffold enforces it for you. In practice this means a few concrete things.

items: Session-level intent classification: run a lightweight classifier or a cheap model call at the start of each session and periodically mid-session to detect domain drift. If a coding agent session pivots toward medical, legal, or financial advice, that should be a detectable signal, not a silent pass-through.

Tool surface restriction: the tools you expose are the sharpest domain constraint available to you. A coding agent that cannot call out to medical imaging APIs or does not receive DICOM input as a registered tool type has a narrower blast radius, even if the underlying model is still willing to reason over images described in text.

Confidence and disclaimer injection: for high-stakes domain drift, a system prompt instruction to surface uncertainty and direct users to qualified professionals is imperfect but not worthless. It changes the epistemic frame the model presents its output in.

Evals at the domain boundary: write test cases that explicitly probe whether your agent will helpfully wander into medical, legal, or financial reasoning when given an opening. If you have never run those evals, you do not know what your system actually does.

content.text: None of this is a reason to slow down on building agentic systems. The MRI story is also a story about genuine utility — someone in an uncertain medical situation felt more informed after the session, and the post was honest about the limitations. That is real value. The engineering takeaway is not that general-purpose agentic scaffolding is dangerous and should be locked down. It is that the domain boundary is your responsibility to define and enforce, because the model will not do it for you, and your users will not do it either. Design for the runtime behavior you actually get, not the use case you intended.

title: LUKS Suspend Stopped Wiping Keys in Linux 6.9 — And Nobody Told You
dek: A silent kernel regression since Linux 6.9 means LUKS suspend no longer scrubs disk-encryption keys from memory. Here is what that breaks, why it slipped through, and what you should do about it.
excerpt: Full-disk encryption with LUKS is supposed to scrub your disk-encryption keys from RAM the moment you suspend. Since Linux 6.9, that scrub quietly stopped happening — no error, no warning, no log entry. Just keys sitting in memory, waiting.

content.alt: LUKS Suspend Stopped Wiping Keys in Linux 6.9 — And Nobody Told You

content.text: Full-disk encryption with LUKS is supposed to scrub your disk-encryption keys from RAM the moment you suspend. Since Linux 6.9, that scrub quietly stopped happening — no error, no warning, no log entry. Just keys sitting in memory, waiting. If you upgraded past 6.9 and never audited your suspend behavior, you have been operating under a threat model that the kernel silently broke.
What LUKS Suspend Is Actually Supposed to Do

LUKS — the Linux Unified Key Setup — sits on top of the dm-crypt kernel module. When you unlock a LUKS volume, the derived encryption key lives in the kernel keyring and gets handed to dm-crypt, which uses it to encrypt and decrypt block I/O transparently. That key is plaintext in kernel memory for as long as the volume is open.

The luks-suspend mechanism exists precisely to close that window during suspend. When you invoke it — either directly or through a hook in your suspend pipeline — it is supposed to freeze all processes, flush pending I/O, hand the encryption key back, scrub it from memory, and only then let the system enter the suspend state. On resume, you re-enter your passphrase, the key is re-derived, and the volume unlocks again. The guarantee is simple: a suspended machine should be cryptographically equivalent to a powered-off one.
Since Linux 6.9, that guarantee is gone. The suspend path proceeds, but the key wipe does not happen. The system suspends with the keys resident in RAM, silently.
Why Leaving Keys in RAM Is Not a Theoretical Problem

content.alt: Why Leaving Keys in RAM Is Not a Theoretical Problem

content.text: Two concrete attack classes make this matter in practice, not just on paper.

The first is cold boot attack. DRAM is not instantly volatile. At room temperature, contents can persist for seconds to several minutes after power is cut. Cooling the modules — even with a can of compressed air held inverted — extends retention significantly. An attacker who can briefly access a suspended or freshly powered-off machine, remove the DIMMs, and move them to a tool that dumps their contents can recover keys that were never scrubbed. This technique is well-documented in academic literature and has been demonstrated against full-disk encryption systems in practice.

The second is direct memory access via Thunderbolt or PCIe. DMA-capable interfaces can read arbitrary physical memory without CPU involvement and without rebooting the machine at all. A device plugged into a Thunderbolt port on a suspended laptop can, absent IOMMU protections, sweep memory and pull key material. IOMMU helps, but configuration is not uniform across distributions and hardware, and it does not protect you if the machine is left in a state where keys were supposed to be gone but were not.

Suspend-to-RAM is the default sleep state on most Linux laptops and on many server configurations. This is not an edge case. S3 is the path every engineer hits when they close the lid or step away from a machine.
How to Check Your Exposure Right Now

content.alt: How to Check Your Exposure Right Now

content.text: Start by confirming your kernel version and whether luks-suspend is even in your suspend pipeline.

content.code:
uname -r

# Check if luks-suspend is installed
which luks-suspend 2>/dev/null || echo "luks-suspend not found"

# On systemd systems, inspect the suspend target hooks
systemctl cat systemd-suspend.service

# List active dm-crypt devices
dmsetup ls --target crypt

# Inspect which keys are held in the kernel keyring
keyctl show @u

content.text: If you are running a kernel at or above 6.9 and relying on luks-suspend for key wiping, you need to treat that wipe as unconfirmed until the regression is addressed upstream or patched in your distribution. Pinning to a kernel below 6.9 is one mitigation, though it trades one risk surface for others. The more conservative posture is to power the machine fully off rather than suspend it until you have confirmed the behavior on your specific kernel build.

Until this is fixed in your kernel, prefer full shutdown over suspend for machines handling sensitive data. If suspend is unavoidable, verify your IOMMU is enforcing, and treat physical access to a suspended machine as equivalent to full compromise.
content.label: Mitigation

content.text: The Deeper Engineering Failure: Security Properties Without Regression Tests

This regression is a systems engineering problem as much as a kernel bug. Security properties are behavioral invariants. They need to be tested with the same rigor as functional correctness — arguably more, because their failure is often invisible. A broken key-wipe does not produce an error code. dm-crypt keeps running. The system resumes normally. The only signal that something is wrong is the absence of a guarantee, and absence is hard to observe.

Kernel CI does an excellent job catching boot failures, performance regressions, and driver breakage. It is far weaker at asserting that a specific memory region was zeroed before a power state transition. That class of property requires targeted tests that instrument the expected side effect — not just that the suspend succeeded, but that the key material is absent from memory afterward. Without that test, a refactor anywhere in the suspend call chain can silently drop the wipe and ship to millions of machines.

The broader principle extends beyond the kernel. Any system that offers a security guarantee — token revocation, session invalidation, secret zeroing — needs a verification path that checks the guarantee was actually upheld, not just that the happy path returned success. Defense in depth only works when each layer is independently verified. When one layer quietly fails, the layers above it look fine from the outside. That is exactly what happened here: LUKS looks intact, dm-crypt is running, the disk is encrypted — and the keys are sitting in RAM.
Check your kernel version. Verify your suspend behavior. Do not trust the abstraction.

title: pgrust: What Passing 100% of Postgres Regression Tests Actually Means
dek: Rewriting Postgres in Rust is not a compiler exercise. It is a behavioral fidelity problem — and clearing every regression test is the most honest signal yet that Rust is ready for foundational systems work.
excerpt: Someone rewrote Postgres in Rust and passed every single regression test. Before you shrug that off as a compiler milestone, let me tell you exactly why that is one of the harder engineering achievements you will read about this year.

content.text: Someone rewrote Postgres in Rust and passed every single regression test. Before you shrug that off as a compiler milestone, let me tell you exactly why that is one of the harder engineering achievements you will read about this year. Postgres is not a side project you can fake your way through. It is thirty-plus years of accumulated correctness, and the regression suite is the only honest judge of whether your rewrite actually understood the assignment.

The project is called pgrust, it is open source at github.com/malisper/pgrust, and the headline is deceptively simple: it passes 100% of Postgres regression tests. People who have not worked deeply with Postgres internals might read that as 'the tests are green, ship it.' Engineers who have spent real time in that codebase will read it differently. They will understand that the regression suite is not a collection of unit tests checking isolated function outputs. It is a behavioral contract covering SQL conformance, type coercion rules, transaction semantics, query planner behavior, extension hooks, and a sprawling mass of edge cases that are not documented anywhere except in the test expectations themselves. Clearing all of it means you did not just implement Postgres. You replicated its personality.
The Regression Suite Is Not a Test Suite. It Is the Spec.

This is the first thing worth internalizing. Postgres does not have a formal specification the way an IETF protocol does. There is no document you can open and derive a ground-truth implementation from. The behavior of the C codebase is the specification. Every quirk in how a type coercion resolves, every specific error message string, every edge in how the planner chooses between two equivalent plans — all of it constitutes the contract your rewrite must honor. The regression tests check exact output. Not approximate output. Not semantically equivalent output. Exact output, down to whitespace and error message wording.

This is the same philosophy that makes SQLite and curl so remarkable as artifacts. The maintainers of those projects long ago stopped thinking of the software as an implementation of an idea and started treating it as the idea itself. You cannot ship a compatible replacement for curl by reading the HTTP RFCs. You ship it by matching curl's actual behavior across thirty years of real-world quirks. pgrust is operating in that same territory. The bar is not correctness in the abstract. The bar is behavioral fidelity to a specific C program that has been running production databases since before most working engineers entered the industry.
Why Mapping C Postgres onto Rust Is a Design Problem, Not a Translation Problem

content.alt: Why Mapping C Postgres onto Rust Is a Design Problem, Not a Translation Problem

content.text: Here is where the technical depth gets interesting. Postgres's C codebase is built around manual memory management in ways that are architectural, not incidental. The memory context system, for example, is a hand-rolled arena allocator that lives at the heart of query execution. Palloc and related primitives let Postgres allocate memory in a context-aware way and then free entire subtrees of allocations at once when a transaction or query scope ends. This is elegant for C, but it does not map cleanly onto Rust's ownership and borrow semantics.

In Rust, the compiler enforces at compile time that a value has exactly one owner, and that references to a value cannot outlive it. This is precisely what prevents entire classes of use-after-free and dangling pointer bugs. But Postgres's memory model leans heavily on arena-style lifetimes that are determined at runtime, not statically. Bridging that gap requires genuine design work. You are not mechanically translating syntax. You are deciding how to express a runtime lifetime discipline inside a type system that wants to reason about lifetimes statically. That means choices about where to use reference counting, where to use raw pointers wrapped in unsafe blocks, and where to restructure ownership entirely so the compiler can track it.

Beyond memory, Postgres carries decades of implicit assumptions in its internals about things like integer overflow behavior, signed-versus-unsigned arithmetic, and the ordering of side effects in expressions. C and Rust have different rules in some of these areas, and a rewrite has to account for each one explicitly. There is no shortcut. Every place where C's undefined behavior was incidentally benign in the original code is a place where the Rust rewrite has to make a deliberate choice and verify that the output still matches.
What This Signals About Rust in Foundational Systems Software

The narrative around Rust for the past several years has leaned heavily on greenfield use cases: new microservices, new CLI tooling, new network proxies. Those are genuine wins, but they do not prove the hardest thing. They do not prove that Rust can replace something you cannot afford to break. pgrust is a proof point in that harder category.

The significance is not just that Rust can express the same logic as C. It is that a team was able to take one of the most complex and behavior-sensitive codebases in existence, rewrite it in a language with fundamentally different memory semantics, and produce something that the existing test suite cannot distinguish from the original. That is a strong statement about Rust's expressiveness for systems-level code. It also says something about the maturity of the ecosystem around unsafe Rust. Writing a database engine requires reaching past the borrow checker in controlled ways, and doing that correctly across a codebase of this complexity is not trivial.

Behavioral fidelity is harder than feature parity. Anyone can list the SQL features they support. Passing the exact output of thirty years of regression tests means you understood the implicit contract, not just the documented one. That is the bar that matters for replacing foundational infrastructure.
content.label: Engineering Takeaway

content.text: The Engineering Lesson Worth Carrying Forward

If you work on systems software, the pgrust milestone deserves attention for a reason beyond the Rust angle. It is a case study in what serious compatibility work actually requires. The team did not set out to build something Postgres-like. They built something that the Postgres test suite accepts as Postgres. That distinction shapes every architectural decision: you cannot optimize in ways that change edge-case output, you cannot clean up error messages, you cannot reorder anything that the tests can observe.

That discipline is what separates a research prototype from a production replacement. It is the same discipline that makes OpenSSL reimplementations so treacherous, or that makes a new filesystem driver so hard to certify. The artifact you are replacing has accumulated behavioral surface area that no one fully mapped out. Your job is to match all of it, including the parts that were accidental. pgrust appears to have done that. Whether it eventually becomes a viable production path for Postgres workloads is a separate question that will depend on performance characteristics, extension compatibility, and operational tooling. But the regression milestone alone is worth pausing on. It is Rust earning its place in the room where foundational software actually lives.

Go read the code at github.com/malisper/pgrust. Even if you never deploy it, the way the codebase handles the memory context problem and the places it reaches for unsafe are an education in what it looks like to take Rust seriously as a systems language rather than as a safer scripting tool. That is the kind of open source work that moves the whole field forward.
